elements.sent-2411-001: Neuroscience and artificial intelligence (AI) both face the challenge of interpreting high-dimensional neural data, where the comparative analysis of such data is crucial for revealing shared mechanisms and differences between these complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite the widespread use of representational comparisons and the abundance classes of comparison methods, a critical question remains: which metrics are most suitable for these comparisons? While some studies evaluate metrics based on their ability to differentiate models of different origins or constructions (e.g., various architectures), another approach is to assess how well they distinguish models that exhibit distinct behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-003: To investigate this, we examine the degree of alignment between various representational similarity measures and behavioral outcomes, employing group statistics and a comprehensive suite of behavioral metrics for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-004: In our evaluation of eight commonly used representational similarity metrics in the visual domain—spanning alignment-based, Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA)-based, inner product kernel-based, and nearest-neighbor methods—we found that metrics like linear Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) and Procrustes distance, which emphasize the overall geometric structure or shape of representations, excelled in differentiating trained from untrained models and aligning with behavioral measures, whereas metrics such as linear predictivity, commonly used in neuroscience, demonstrated only moderate alignment with behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: These insights are crucial for selecting metrics that emphasize behaviorally meaningful comparisons in NeuroAI research.
elements.sent-2411-006: Both neuroscience and artificial intelligence (AI) confront the challenge of high-dimensional neural data, whether from neurobiological firing rates, voxel responses, or hidden layer activations in artificial networks.
elements.sent-2411-007: Comparing such high-dimensional neural data is critical for both fields, as it facilitates understanding of complex systems by revealing their underlying similarities and differences.
elements.sent-2411-008: In neuroscience, one of the main goals is to uncover how neural activity drives behavior and to understand neural computations at an algorithmic level.
elements.sent-2411-009: Comparisons across species and between brain and model representations, particularly those of deep neural networks, have been instrumental in advancing this understanding~(\cite{yamins2014performance,eickenberg2017seeing,gucclu2015deep,cichy2016comparison,KriegeskorteRSA, schrimpf2018brain, schrimpf2020integrative, storrs2021diverse, kriegeskorte2008representational ).}
elements.sent-2411-010: A growing interest lies in systematically altering model parameters—such as architecture, learning objectives, and training data—and comparing the resulting internal representations with neural data~(\cite{yamins2016using, doerig2023neuroconnectionist, schrimpf2018brain, schrimpf2020integrative ).}
elements.sent-2411-011: Similarly, in AI, researchers are increasingly focused on reverse-engineering neural networks by tweaking architectural components, training objectives, and data inputs to examine how these modifications impact the resulting representations.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, studying neural networks in isolation can be limiting, as interactions between the learning algorithms and structured data shape these systems in ways we do not yet fully understand.
elements.sent-2411-013: Comparative analysis of model representations offers a powerful tool to probe these networks more deeply.
elements.sent-2411-014: This endeavor is rooted in the universality hypothesis that similar phenomena can arise across different networks.
elements.sent-2411-015: Indeed, a large number of studies have provided empirical evidence licensing these universal theories~(\cite{huh2024platonic, kornblith2019similarity, bansal2021revisiting, li2015convergent, roeder2021linear, lenc2015understanding ) but the extent to which diverse neural networks converge to similar representations is not well understood.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Given the growing interest in comparative analyses across neuroscience and AI, a key question arises: what are the best tools for conducting such analyses? Over the past decade, a wide variety of approaches have emerged for quantifying the representational similarity across artificial and biological neural representations~(\cite{gettingaligned, klabunde2023similarity, williams2021generalized ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Most of these approaches can be classified as belonging to one of four categories: representational similarity based measures, alignment-based measures, nearest-neighbor based measures and canonical correlation analysis-based measures~(\cite{klabunde2023similarity ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: With the wide range of available approaches for representational comparisons, researchers are tasked with selecting a suitable metric.
elements.sent-2411-019: The choice of a specific metric implicitly prioritizes certain properties of the system, as different approaches emphasize distinct invariances and are sensitive to varying aspects of the representations.
elements.sent-2411-020: This complexity ties into broader issues in the concept and assessment of similarity, which, as emphasized in psychology, is highly context-dependent~(\cite{tversky1977features ).}
elements.sent-2411-021: What, then, are the key desiderata for network comparison metrics? Networks may exhibit similarities in some dimensions and differences in others, but the critical question is whether these differences are functionally relevant or merely reflect differences in origin or construction.
elements.sent-2411-022: This consideration leads to a central criterion for effective metrics: behavioral differences should correspond to differences in internal representational similarity~(\cite{cao2022putting ).}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, identifying which measures reliably capture behaviorally meaningful differences remains an open question.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our study aims to address the above challenge.
elements.sent-2411-025: Here, we make the following key contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-026: We conduct an extensive analysis of common representational comparison measures (including alignment-based, representational similarity matrix-based, CCA-based, and nearest-neighbor-based methods) and show that these measures differ in their capacity to distinguish between models.
elements.sent-2411-027: While some measures excel at distinguishing between models from different architectural families, others are better at separating trained from untrained models.
elements.sent-2411-028: To assess which of these distinctions reflects differences in model behaviors, we perform complementary behavioral comparisons using a comprehensive set of behavioral metrics (both hard and soft prediction-based).
elements.sent-2411-029: We find that behavioral metrics are generally more consistent with each other than representational similarity measures.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we cross-compare representational and behavioral similarity measures, revealing that linear CKA and Procrustes distance align most closely with behavioral evaluations, whereas metrics like linear predictivity, widely used in neuroscience, show only modest alignment.
elements.sent-2411-031: This finding offers important guidance for metric selection in neuroAI, where the functional relevance of representational comparisons is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-032: Although few studies directly compare representational similarity measures based on their discriminative power, most efforts in this area focus on identifying metrics that distinguish between models by their construction.
elements.sent-2411-033: These efforts typically involve assessing measures based on their ability to match corresponding layers across models with varying seeds~\citep{kornblith2019similarity or identical architectures with different initializations~\citep{han2023system, rahamim2024contrasim .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The closest to our work are studies by Ding et al.~\citep{ding2021grounding and Cloos et al.~\citep{cloos2024differentiable .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Cloos et al.~\citep{cloos2024differentiable optimized synthetic datasets to resemble brain activity under various measures, demonstrating that metrics like linear predictivity and CKA can yield high scores even when task-relevant variables are not encoded.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Ding et al.~(\citep{ding2021grounding ) examined the sensitivity of representational similarity measures—CCA, CKA, and Procrustes—in BERT models (NLP) and ResNet models (CIFAR-10) to factors that either preserve functional behavior (e.g., random seed variations) or alter it (e.g., principal component deletion).}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, these studies examine a limited set of similarity measures and primarily assess functional similarity based on task performance alone, without evaluating the finer-grained alignment of predictions across models.
elements.sent-2411-038: Let \( S \) be a set of \( M \) fixed input stimuli.
elements.sent-2411-039: Define the kernel functions \footnote{The term "encoder/kernel function: refers to the function that represents the mapping from an input to the output of a specific layer's activation in a neural network \( f: S \to \mathbb{R ^{N_X \) and \( g: S \to \mathbb{R ^{N_Y \), where \( N_X \) and \( N_Y \) are the output unit sizes of the first and second encoders, respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Here, \( f(s_i) \) and \( g(s_i) \) map each stimulus \( s_i \in S \) to vectors in \( \mathbb{R ^{N_X \) and \( \mathbb{R ^{N_Y \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Let \( X \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times N_X \) and \( Y \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times N_Y \) be the representation matrices.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: For each input stimulus \( s_i \), denote the \( i \)th row of \( X \) as \( \phi_i = f(s_i) \) and of \( Y \) as \( \psi_i = g(s_i) \), each being the activation in response to the \( i \)th stimulus.
elements.sent-2411-043: The Kendall rank correlation coefficient \( \tau(\cdot) \) quantifies the similarity between these RDMs.
elements.sent-2411-044: Here, the $i^{th}$ canonical correlation coefficient $\rho_i$ (associated with the $i^{th}$ optimized canonical weights $w_x^i \in \mathbb{R ^{N_X}$ and $w_y^i \in \mathbb{R ^{N_Y}$) is being calculated by: \begin{equation}\rho_i = \max_{w_x^i, w_y^i} corr(X w_x^i, Y w_y^i)\end{equation} \begin{equation}subject to  \forall j < i, \quad X w_x^i \perp X w_x^j \quad and \quad Y w_y^i \perp Y w_y^j,\end{equation} with the transformed matrices $X w_x^i$ and $Y w_y^i$ being called canonical variables.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}subject to  \forall j < i, \quad X w_x^i \perp X w_x^j \quad and \quad Y w_y^i \perp Y w_y^j,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: To obtain a measure of similarity between neural network representations, the mean CCA correlation coefficient $\bar{\rho}$ over the first $N'$ components is reported, with $N'=\min(N_X,N_Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Here, \begin{equation}\bar{\rho} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N'} \rho_i}{N'} = \frac{\left\| Q_Y^T Q_X \right\|_*}{N'},\end{equation} where $\|\cdot\|_*$ denotes the nuclear norm.
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, \(Q_X = X(X^T X)^{-1/2}\) and \(Q_Y = Y(Y^T Y)^{-1/2}\) represent any orthonormal bases for the columns of \(X\) and \(Y\).
elements.sent-2411-048: This measure possesses a weaker invariance assumption than CCA, being invariant only to orthogonal transformations, rather than all classes of invertible linear transformations, which implies the preservation of scalar products and Euclidean distances between pairs of stimuli. \begin{equation}CKA(K, L) = \frac{HSIC(K, L)}{\sqrt{HSIC(K, K) HSIC(L, L)}}\end{equation} with \( K \) and \( L \) be kernel matrices where \( K_{ij = \kappa(\phi_i, \phi_j) \) and \( L_{ij = \kappa(\psi_i, \psi_j) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-049: These matrices represent the inner products of vectorized features \( \phi \) and \( \psi \) from two different models, respectively, computed using the kernel function function \( \kappa \).
elements.sent-2411-050: In the linear case, \( \kappa \) is the inner product, implying $K=XX^T, L=YY^T$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion $\text{HSIC (\cdot)$ evaluates the cross-covariance of the models' internal embedding spaces, focusing on the similarity of stimulus pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-052: An asymmetric measure of alignment between the representations of two systems, obtained using ridge regression.
elements.sent-2411-053: The numerical score is calculated by summing Pearson's correlations between each pair of predicted and actual activations in the held-out set.
elements.sent-2411-054: For reporting, we provide symmetrized scores by averaging the correlation coefficients from both fitting directions.
elements.sent-2411-055: Here, the Procrustes distance is given by: \begin{equation}d(X,Y) = \min_{T \in O(n)} \|\phi(X) - \phi(Y)T\|_F\end{equation} where \(\phi(\cdot)\) is the function that whitens the covariance of the matrix X and Y, i.e. the columns sum to zero and $\|\phi(X)\|_F, \|\phi(X)\|_F = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-056: The similarity scores reported are obtained by \(1 - d(X,Y)\), such that the comparison with a representation itself yields a score of 1, and lower distance yields a higher score.
elements.sent-2411-057: The scores reported are the average from both fitting directions. \begin{equation}s_semi(X, Y) = \frac{1}{N_x} \sum_{i=1}^{N_x} \max_{j \in \{1, \dots, N_y\}} x_i^\top y_j\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: It measures alignment by relaxing the set of permutations to ``soft permutations''.
elements.sent-2411-059: Specifically, consider a nonnegative matrix $\mathbf{P \in \reals^{N_x \times N_y}$ whose rows each sum to $1 / N_x$ and whose columns each sum to $1 / N_y$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The set of all such matrices defines a transportation polytope~\citep{de2013combinatorics , denoted as $\mathrm{T (N_x, N_y)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Optimizing over this set of rectangular matrices results in a ``soft matching'' or ``soft permutation'' of neuron labels in the sense that every row and column of $\mathbf{P}$ may have more than one non-zero element. \begin{equation}d_{T}(X, Y) = \sqrt{\min_{P \in T(N_X, N_Y)} \sum_{i,j} P_{ij} \|x_i - y_j\|^2}\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{figs/schematic_rep_eval.png} \caption{Framework for evaluating representational similarity metrics based on their functional correspondence. We conduct pairwise comparisons of the representational similarities and behavioral outputs of 19 vision models, utilizing 9 widely-used representational similarity measures and 10 behavioral metrics across 17 distinct behavioral datasets.} \label{figs:fig1}
elements.sent-2411-062: For classification tasks, we incorporate various downstream measurements at different levels of granularity to assess behavioral consistency across systems.
elements.sent-2411-063: For a given pair of neural networks, their activations over a shared set of stimuli are extracted.
elements.sent-2411-064: A linear readout based on a fully connected layer is trained over a training set of activations, where the resulting behavioral classification decisions determined by the linear readouts on a held-out testing set are exploited in the following ways as a comparison between the neural networks:.
elements.sent-2411-065: Compares two models' representations by their linear-readout's softmax layer activation, which is a class-dimensional vector reflecting the model's judgement of the probabilities assigned to each label for a given input, with scores calculated by summing the Pearson correlation coefficient between these softmax vectors over the testing set.
elements.sent-2411-066: A similarity score is obtained by comparing the two models' confusion matrices in the following ways:.
elements.sent-2411-067: For class-wise JS distance, let \( \hat{p = \langle p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C \rangle \) and \( \hat{q = \langle q_1, q_2, \dots, q_C \rangle \) be error probability vectors over C classes, with \begin{equation}p_i = \frac{e_i}{\sum_{i=1}^C e_i}, \forall i \in \{1,2,...,C\}\end{equation} where \( e_i \) represents error counts per class.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The JS divergence is defined as: \begin{equation}JSD(p, q) = \sqrt{\frac{D(p || m) + D(q || m)}{2}},\end{equation} \begin{equation}with  D(p || m) = \sum_{i=1}^C p_i \log\left(\frac{p_i}{m_i}\right) and  m_i = \frac{p_i + q_i}{2}\end{equation} A finer inter-class dissimilarity measure derived from the complete misclassification patterns shown in the non-diagonal elements of the confusion matrix results in two \(C*(C-1)\) dimensional flattened vectors \(\hat{p}\) and \(\hat{q}\), where each component is proportional to the counts of misclassifications from class \(i\) to class \(j\), is calculated as \begin{equation}\frac{e_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^C \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^C e_{ij}}, \quad \forall i, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}\end{equation} The resulting distances from both method range from [0, 1], where we simply report a similarity measure given by $1- JSD(p,q)$.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}with  D(p || m) = \sum_{i=1}^C p_i \log\left(\frac{p_i}{m_i}\right) and  m_i = \frac{p_i + q_i}{2}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\frac{e_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^C \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^C e_{ij}}, \quad \forall i, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-069: The error patterns for each model are encoded as vectors of binary values, where each entry corresponds to the correctness of a stimulus's prediction.
elements.sent-2411-070: We incorporate the following measures to compare alignment between the binary vectors:.
elements.sent-2411-071: Cohen's \(\kappa\) assesses the consistency of error overlap, providing a measure of classification agreement without distinguishing error types.
elements.sent-2411-072: The numerator "\(|\text{Intersections |\)" counts samples where both models predict correctly, normalized by "\(|\text{Unions |\)", which counts samples where either model predicts correctly.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: We analyze the behavior of all models across a series of downstream tasks, including in-distribution and several out-of-distribution image types, such as silhouettes, stylized images, and natural images distorted by various noise types (see Appendix~\ref{subsec:datasets for details).}
elements.sent-2411-074: In total, these comparisons span 17 behavioral datasets.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Selection of Neural Network Architectures and Layers
elements.sent-2411-075: We incorporated a comprehensive list of popular deep learning models pretrained over the 1000-class classification tasks over the ImageNet-1k dataset \citep{imagenet .}
elements.sent-2411-076: The selection spans a diverse set of architectures, including conventional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformers.
elements.sent-2411-077: These models were trained using various objective functions, both supervised and self-supervised.
elements.sent-2411-078: Specifically, our lineup includes AlexNet \citep{alexnet , ResNet ~\citep{resnet2015 , VGG16 ~\citep{vgg , Inception ~\citep{inception , ResNeXt ~\citep{resnext , MoCo \citep{moco , ResNet Robust \citep{robustness , and several variants of Vision Transformers (ViTs) ~\citep{vit2020 such as Vit-b16 and ViT-ResNet (vit on ResNet architecture), and Swin transformer \citep{swin .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: For representational analysis, we mainly focused on the penultimate layer of each model, where we averaged the outputs across channels or patches, as applicable per architecture.
elements.sent-2411-080: For transformer models, we've included outputs from the final GELU activation layers in addition to their penultimate layer.
elements.sent-2411-081: We included randomized versions of AlexNet, ResNet, ViT, and Swin to study their behavior under random initialization before training.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/model_sim_viz.png} \caption{Model-by-model similarity matrices from different measures on the Cue Conflict task. Left: The Procrustes measure clearly distinguishes between trained and untrained models. Middle: Linear Predictivity reveals no noticeable separation between trained and untrained models or across different architectures. Right: Soft-matching more effectively differentiates between architectural families (CNN vs. transformers) compared to other representational metrics.} \label{figs:fig2a}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Different Representational Similarity Measures have Distinct Capacities for Model Separation
elements.sent-2411-082: To characterize how different representational similarity measures discriminate models, we first visualize the model-by-model similarity matrices for each measure.
elements.sent-2411-083: We observed that while some measures like the soft-matching distance were effective at differentiating architectural families (Fig. \ref{figs:fig2a , right), others like the Procrustes distance were more sensitive to the effects of training (Fig. \ref{figs:fig2a , left), clearly separating trained from untrained models.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Other measures, like linear predictivity, which allow greater flexibility in aligning the two representations, showed limited ability in distinguishing between models trained with different architectures or trained from untrained models (see Appendix~\ref{subsec:rsms for additional similarity matrices).}
elements.sent-2411-085: To quantify these distinctions, we computed $d'$ scores (Appendix~\ref{subsec:dprime ) to assess each measure's ability to differentiate two categories of models: (a) those from different architectural families, and (b) those with varying levels of training (trained vs. untrained).}
elements.sent-2411-086: Significant differences in $d'$ scores emerged across measures (Fig. \ref{figs:fig2b ).}
elements.sent-2411-087: For instance, Procrustes achieved $d'$ scores with a mean of 3.70 when separating trained from untrained models across all datasets, while commonly used measures like CCA and linear predictivity produced much lower scores with means of 0.53 and 0.87, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-088: Similarly, some measures were better at discriminating architectural differences, with the soft-matching distance demonstrating the highest discriminability (mean of $d'$ scores = 1.6).
elements.sent-2411-089: Previous studies have also demonstrated that different measures vary in their effectiveness at establishing layer-wise correspondence across networks with the same architecture~\citep{kornblith2019similarity, thobani2024inter .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Considering these differences in how measures distinguish between models, a key question emerges: Which distinctions should we prioritize?.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figs/dprimes} \caption{Discriminative ability (d' scores) of (top) representational and (bottom) behavioral similarity measures in distinguishing between trained vs. untrained models (left) and architectures (right).} \label{figs:fig2b}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Behavioral Metrics Primarily Reflect Learning Differences Over Architectural Variations
elements.sent-2411-091: To address the question of which separation should be prioritized, we return to our central premise: measures that emphasize functional distinctions should be favored.
elements.sent-2411-092: Therefore, we next evaluated how different behavioral measures (as previously described) distinguish between models.
elements.sent-2411-093: Our results show that behavioral metrics effectively and consistently separate trained from untrained networks, with even the weakest metric (Confusion Matrix (JSD)) achieving a mean $d'$ of 1.82.
elements.sent-2411-094: However, most behavioral measures struggle to differentiate between architectural families (e.g., CNNs vs. Transformers), with the best-performing metric (Confusion Matrix (Inter-class JSD)) achieving an average $d'$ of 0.65 across all behavioral datasets (see Appendix~\ref{subsec:behavmatrices for all similarity matrices).}
elements.sent-2411-095: This suggests that differences in these architectural motifs have minimal impact on model behavior.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Behavioral Metrics Show Greater Consistency Than Neural Representational Similarity Measures
elements.sent-2411-096: We next examined the consistency across different representational similarity measures and across different behavioral measures by computing correlations between the model-by-model similarity matrices generated by each measure.
elements.sent-2411-097: As shown in Fig. \ref{figs:fig3 (Top),we find that behavioral metrics (mean r: $0.85 \pm 0.01$) are more correlated on average than representational metrics (mean r: $0.75 \pm 0.007$), with a significant difference $(z = -7.10, p = 5 \times 10^{-8 < 0.0001)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: To further understand the relationships between different representational similarity measures, we analyzed the MDS plot (Fig. \ref{figs:fig3 (Bottom)).}
elements.sent-2411-099: This visualization revealed distinct clusters of measures based on their theoretical properties.
elements.sent-2411-100: Measures that rely on inner product kernels (stimulus-by-stimulus dissimilarities) tend to group together, indicating they capture similar aspects of representational structure.
elements.sent-2411-101: On the other hand, measures that use explicit, direct mappings between individual neurons—such as Linear Predictivity and Semi-Matching—form a separate cluster.
elements.sent-2411-102: Notably, Procrustes Distance and CCA also involve alignment, similar to Linear Predictivity and Semi-Matching; however, this alignment is achieved collectively across all units or neurons rather than through independently determined mappings for each neuron.
elements.sent-2411-103: Procrustes aligns the entire configuration of points, while CCA projects the two representations onto common subspaces to maximize correlation, further distinguishing them from other representational similarity approaches.
elements.sent-2411-104: How behavioral metrics distinguish models is crucial, as most comparative analyses of representations in neuroscience and AI revolve around understanding computations and how those computations relate to behavior; behaviorally grounded comparisons of model representations are key to this endeavor.
elements.sent-2411-105: We find that behavioral metrics distinguish between models in a consistent manner across different datasets, reinforcing the robustness of the model relationships they uncover (Appendix ~\ref{subsec:consistency ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: The consistency of the behavioral metrics -across datasets and with each other- fulfills another scientific desiderata of replicability.
elements.sent-2411-107: Therefore, the model relationships identified by behavioral metrics are not only important but also reliable.
elements.sent-2411-108: It becomes crucial, then, to determine which representational similarity measures align with these robust behavioral relationships between models.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/mds_metric_corr.png} \caption{Consistency Between Similarity Metrics. (A) and (C) display the correlation matrix averaged across all behavioral datasets and the 2D-projected multidimensional scaling (MDS) plot (using 1 minus the correlation matrix as the distance matrix) for behavioral measures. (B) and (D) illustrate the average correlation matrix and the MDS plot for representational similarity measures.} \label{figs:fig3}
elements.subsection-2411-008: Which representational similarity measures show the strongest correspondence with behavioral measures?
elements.sent-2411-109: Seeing that we want to prioritize the model relationships uncovered by behavioral metrics, we move on to investigate which --if any-- representational similarity metrics reveal the same underlying relationships between models.
elements.sent-2411-110: To rigorously assess this, we computed correlations between the model-by-model similarity matrices of each representational metric with the model-by-model behavioral similarity matrix averaged across all behavioral metrics, separately for many datasets (Fig \ref{figs:fig4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-111: We found that three metrics stood out in their alignment with behavioral metrics - RSA (mean r: $0.52$), Linear CKA (mean r: $0.64$), and Procrustes (mean r: $0.70$).
elements.sent-2411-112: Going back to our original analysis, these metrics are also able to more strongly differentiate trained and untrained models (Fig \ref{figs:fig1 Top d' measures).}
elements.sent-2411-113: All these representational metrics emphasize alignment in either the overall geometry or shape of representations.
elements.sent-2411-114: Alternate measures like linear predictivity and CCA, which are commonly employed in representational comparisons in neuroscience and AI, showed significantly weaker alignment with mean correlation scores of $0.26$ and $0.19$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-115: Given the opacity of neural representations, selecting appropriate representational similarity metrics can be challenging; these findings offer crucial guidance for metrics that support behaviorally grounded comparisons.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/metric_gt_corr_figure.png} \caption{Granular Comparison of Representational Similarity Measures with Behavioral Measures: (A) Average correlation between representational and behavioral metrics across datasets. (B) Distribution of correlation scores for each representational similarity measure with behavioral measures; each point represents the averaged score for a dataset across all behavioral measures.} \label{figs:fig4}
elements.sent-2411-116: In this study, we compared 8 neural representational similarity metrics and 9 behavioral measures across 17 datasets.Based on the premise that behavioral differences should be mirrored in the representational structure of neural networks, we examined practical distinctions in their alignment with behavior.
elements.sent-2411-117: Metrics like RSA, CKA, and Procrustes distance, which preserve the overall geometry of neural representations, tend to align closely with behavioral measures.
elements.sent-2411-118: In contrast, methods like linear predictivity, which align dimensions without preserving global geometry, show weaker alignment.
elements.sent-2411-119: This divergence likely arises because linear predictivity has the capacity of mapping complex, distributed geometric structures to simpler, compressed ones while maintaining prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-120: For instance, trained networks were observed to predict untrained network activation patterns well, yielding high symmetrized scores.
elements.sent-2411-121: Moreover, while different behavioral measures generally show consistency, neural representational similarity metrics do not, underscoring the need for a deeper understanding of how these representational metrics discriminate between models in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-122: Our analysis sets a new standard for representational similarity measures in neuroscience and AI, using downstream behavioral robustness as a guide for selecting the most suitable metric.
elements.sent-2411-123: This framework is especially crucial in model-brain comparisons, where representational analyses are frequently applied to assess if artificial neural networks and biological systems are serving comparable functional roles in terms of perceptual and cognitive processes.
elements.sent-2411-124: Our framework for representational metric selection, though robust, makes some key assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-125: It assumes a specific mechanism for how behavior is `reading out' from neural representations, and different readout mechanisms could reveal qualitatively different relationships between models.
elements.sent-2411-126: For example, applying biologically-inspired constraints, such as sparsity, could reveal divergent relationships, especially if some models encode behaviorally relevant information in a sparse manner that others do not.
elements.sent-2411-127: In such cases, the precise representation structure at the unit-level becomes critical.
elements.sent-2411-128: Additionally, we defined "behavior" within the scope of object classification across multiple out-of-distribution (OOD) image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-129: Extending evaluations to include fine-grained visual discrimination or broader tasks beyond categorization would better capture the full range of visual processing.
elements.sent-2411-130: Lastly, a stronger theoretical framework explaining why certain similarity measures align more closely with behavior than others is currently lacking in our work, but this remains an exciting direction for future research.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
elements.sent-2411-131: All datasets, directly drawn from~\cite{geirhos2019, wang2019learning, geirhos2021partial , share the coarser 16 labels from ImageNet.}
elements.sent-2411-132: These consist of a subset of the ImageNet1k validation set sampled from the following categories: Airplane, Bear, Bicycle, Bird, Boat, Bottle, Car, Cat, Chair, Clock, Dog, Elephant, Keyboard, Knife, Oven, Truck.
elements.sent-2411-133: Colour: Served as a baseline in-distribution dataset, with half of the images randomly converted to greyscale and the rest kept in original color.
elements.sent-2411-134: Includes a total of 1280 images (80 images per label).
elements.sent-2411-135: Stylized ImageNet (SIN): Textures from one class are applied to shapes from another while maintaining object shapes.
elements.sent-2411-136: Shape labels are used as "true labels" for confusion matrix and correctness analyses.
elements.sent-2411-138: Sketch: Contains cartoon-styled sketches of objects from each class, totaling 800 images.
elements.sent-2411-139: Edges: Created from the original dataset using the Canny edge extractor for edge-based representations.
elements.sent-2411-141: Silhouette: Black objects on a white background, generated from the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-142: Cue Conflict: Images with texture conflicting with shape category, generated using iterative style transfer ~\citep{gatys2016 between Texture dataset images (style) and Original dataset images (content).}
elements.sent-2411-144: Contrast: Variants of images adjusted for contrast levels.
elements.sent-2411-145: High-Pass/Low-Pass: Images filtered to emphasize either high-frequency or low-frequency components using Gaussian filters.
elements.sent-2411-146: Includes a total of 1280 images per dataset.
elements.sent-2411-147: Phase-Scrambling: Images had phase noise added to frequencies, creating different levels of distortion from 0 to 180 degrees.
elements.sent-2411-149: Power-Equalisation: Images were processed to equalize the power spectra across the dataset by setting all amplitude spectra to their mean value.
elements.sent-2411-150: False-Colour: Images had colors inverted to their opponent colors while keeping luminance constant using the DKL color space.
elements.sent-2411-151: Rotation: Images are rotated by 0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees to test rotational invariant robustness.
elements.sent-2411-152: Eidolon I, II, III: Images distorted using the Eidolon toolbox, varying coherence and reach parameters to manipulate local and global image structures.
elements.sent-2411-153: Each filtering intensity level contains 1280 images.
elements.sent-2411-154: Uniform Noise: White uniform noise added to images with a varying range to assess robustness; pixel values exceeding bounds were clipped.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Inter vs Intra Group Statistic Measures using $d'$ Scores
elements.sent-2411-155: To quantify a comparative metric's ability to reflect the expected proximity between similarly trained models, compared to their dissimilarity with the untrained models, involves speculating the group statistics from the resulting similarity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-156: We employ the $d'$ score defined as: \begin{equation}d' = \frac{\mu(A) - \mu(B)}{\sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2_A + \sigma^2_B}{2}}}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A}$ represents the set of similarity scores from intra-group comparisons, specifically the similarity scores between every pair of trained models.
elements.sent-2411-157: Equivalent to the set of entries located at the intersection of trained model rows and untrained model columns in the model-by-model similarity matrix of the metrics.
elements.sent-2411-158: A similarity metric with \(d'\geq 0\) of greater magnitude indicates a greater ability to separate trained models from untrained ones.
elements.sent-2411-159: A metric with \(d'= 0\) or \(d'< 0\) indicates that there were no discernible difference in average similarity scores computed in "trained model pairs" and "trained vs. untrained model pairs", or that trained vs. untrained models exhibit even higher similarity than that among trained models.
elements.sent-2411-160: Similarly, when examining architectural differences, \( \mathbf{A \) represents intra-group comparisons within Convolutional models, while \( \mathbf{B \) captures inter-group comparisons between Convolutional models and Transformers.}}
elements.sent-2411-161: To assess consistency across behavioral datasets, we used an \(M \times M\) correlation matrix, where \(M\) is the number of datasets.
elements.sent-2411-162: Each entry \(i, j\) represents the correlation between datasets \(i\) and \(j\), derived from their downstream similarity matrices.
elements.sent-2411-163: Averaging these scores across all behavioral measures revealed high correlations, indicating consistent uniformity across most datasets.
elements.sent-2411-164: We include the Model-by-Model Similarity Matrix given by the 8 distinct representation measures.
elements.sent-2411-165: The scores provided are averaged across 17 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-166: For mutual k-NN, different neighborhood sizes (\(k\)) are included.
elements.sent-2411-167: Note that the "\(1-\text{Procrustes}\)" score can range from \((- \infty, 1]\), whereas all other metrics yield scores within the range \([0,1]\).
elements.sent-2411-168: Similarly, we include the Model-by-Model Similarity Matrix given by the 9 distinct behavioral measures.
elements.sent-2411-169: The scores are averaged across 17 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-170: For the measures "\(1-\text{Hamming Distance}\)" and "Agreement Scores", the alignment value can all range from \((- \infty, 1]\), whereas all other measures yield scores within the range \([0,1]\).
elements.sent-2411-171: Comparative analysis of such data is essential to uncover shared mechanisms and differences between these complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-172: Despite the widespread use of representational comparisons and the ever-growing landscape of comparison methods, a critical question remains: which metrics are most suitable for these comparisons? Prior work often evaluates metrics by their ability to differentiate models with varying origins (e.g., different architectures), but an alternative—and arguably more informative—approach is to assess how well these metrics distinguish models with distinct behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-173: This is crucial as representational comparisons are frequently interpreted as indicators of functional similarity in NeuroAI.
elements.sent-2411-174: To investigate this, we examine the degree of alignment between various representational similarity measures and behavioral outcomes in a suite of different downstream data distributions and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-175: We compared eight commonly used metrics in the visual domain, including alignment-based, CCA-based, inner product kernel-based, and nearest-neighbor-based methods, using group statistics and a comprehensive set of behavioral metrics.
elements.sent-2411-176: We found that metrics like the Procrustes distance and linear Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), which emphasize alignment in the overall shape or geometry of representations, excelled in differentiating trained from untrained models and aligning with behavioral measures, whereas metrics such as linear predictivity, commonly used in neuroscience, demonstrated only moderate alignment with behavior.
elements.sent-2411-177: These findings highlight that some widely used representational similarity metrics may not directly map onto functional behaviors or computational goals, underscoring the importance of selecting metrics that emphasize behaviorally meaningful comparisons in NeuroAI research.
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{center} \includegraphics[scale=0.5]{figs/schematic_rep_eval.png} \end{center} \caption{Framework for evaluating representational similarity metrics based on their functional correspondence. We conduct pairwise comparisons of the representational similarities and behavioral outputs of 19 vision models, utilizing 9 widely-used representational similarity measures and 10 behavioral metrics across 20 distinct behavioral datasets. (Top Left) Label-Preserving OOD Datasets (In-Task Distribution Shifts): Maintain ImageNet labels but alter input distributions. (Bottom Left) Task-Shifting Datasets (Out-of-Task Distribution Shifts): Introduce new tasks and labels, modifying both input distributions and task structures.} \label{figs:fig1}
elements.sent-2411-178: Most of these approaches can be classified as belonging to one of four categories: representational similarity-based measures, alignment-based measures, nearest-neighbor based measures, and canonical correlation analysis-based measures~(\cite{klabunde2023similarity ).}
elements.sent-2411-179: This question of whether a similarity metric captures functionally meaningful differences is particularly consequential in NeuroAI, where model–brain comparisons are frequently used to infer the computational objectives operative in the brain.
elements.sent-2411-180: For example,~\cite{yamins2016using demonstrate that “goal-driven deep learning models optimized for challenging object classification tasks produce neural response patterns that quantitatively match those observed in mid-level cortical areas,” using this alignment to generate functional hypotheses about sensory processing.}
elements.sent-2411-181: In the language domain,~\cite{schrimpf2021neural found that models performing best on next-word prediction also excel in explaining human neural data, leading them to conclude that language-selective cortical regions are functionally tuned for predictive processing in service of meaning extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Complementing these findings,~\cite{richards2019deep argue for a broader ``deep learning framework for neuroscience'' that prioritizes objective functions, learning rules, and architectures, emphasizing that systematically comparing model representations with brain data is key to uncovering the computational goals of neural circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-183: In each of these cases, the core assumption is that if a model’s representation aligns with brain data under a particular metric, then the model’s functional objective—such as accurate categorization or predictive processing—mirrors that of the neural circuit in question.
elements.sent-2411-184: Yet the confidence we can place in these conclusions hinges critically on whether the chosen similarity measure truly isolates behaviorally relevant structure, underscoring the importance of identifying metrics that reliably capture functionally meaningful representational differences.
elements.sent-2411-185: This finding offers important guidance for the selection of metric in neuroAI, where the functional relevance of representational comparisons is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-186: Although few studies directly compare representational similarity measures based on their discriminative power, most efforts focus on identifying metrics that distinguish between models by their construction.
elements.sent-2411-187: These efforts typically involve assessing measures based on their ability to align corresponding layers across models with varying seeds~\citep{kornblith2019similarity or identical architectures with different initializations~\citep{han2023system, rahamim2024contrasim or their ability to reliably separate neural responses from distinct brain areas while grouping those from the same area~\citep{thobani2024inter .}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: Closest to our work are~\citet{ding2021grounding and~\citet{cloos2024differentiable .}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The latter optimize synthetic datasets to match brain activity under different metrics, showing that even when task-relevant variables are not encoded, metrics like linear predictivity and CKA can still produce high scores.
elements.sent-2411-190: The former evaluate the sensitivity of CCA, CKA, and Procrustes to perturbations that preserve or disrupt functional behavior (e.g., seed variation, principal component deletion) in BERT (NLP) and ResNet (CIFAR-10).
elements.sent-2411-191: Define the kernel functions \( f: S \to \mathbb{R ^{N_X \) and \( g: S \to \mathbb{R ^{N_Y \), where \( N_X \) and \( N_Y \) are the output unit sizes of the first and second systems.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: This measure possesses a weaker invariance assumption than CCA, being invariant only to orthogonal transformations, rather than all classes of invertible linear transformations, which implies the preservation of scalar products and Euclidean distances between pairs of stimuli. \begin{equation}CKA(K, L) = \frac{HSIC(K, L)}{\sqrt{HSIC(K, K) HSIC(L, L)}}\end{equation} Here, \(K\) and \(L\) are kernel matrices with entries \(K_{ij = \kappa(\phi_i, \phi_j)\) and \(L_{ij = \kappa(\psi_i, \psi_j)\), where \(\phi\) and \(\psi\) are vectorized features from the two models.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: In the linear case, \(\kappa\) is the inner product, so \(K = XX^\top\) and \(L = YY^\top\).
elements.sent-2411-194: HSIC evaluates the cross-covariance of the models' internal embedding spaces, focusing on the similarity of stimulus pairs.
elements.sent-2411-195: For reporting, we symmetrize by averaging correlation scores from both fitting directions.
elements.sent-2411-196: Here, the Procrustes distance is given by: \begin{equation}d(X,Y) = \min_{T \in O(n)} \|\phi(X) - \phi(Y)T\|_F\end{equation} where \(\phi(\cdot)\) denotes centering the matrix (subtracting the column-wise mean so the data is centered at the origin) and scaling it to unit Frobenius norm.
elements.sent-2411-198: For a given pair of neural networks, their activations are extracted over a shared set of stimuli.
elements.sent-2411-199: Similarity is computed as the Pearson correlation between these vectors across the test set.
elements.sent-2411-200: A similarity score is obtained by comparing the two models' confusion matrices in the following ways: {Pearson Correlation Coefficient between the flattened confusion matrices given by two models, each being a vector of dimension $C^2$ over $C$ classes.}
elements.sent-2411-201: The JS divergence is defined as: \begin{equation}JSD(p, q) = \sqrt{\frac{D(p || m) + D(q || m)}{2}},\end{equation} \begin{equation}with  D(p || m) = \sum_{i=1}^C p_i \log\left(\frac{p_i}{m_i}\right) and  m_i = \frac{p_i + q_i}{2}\end{equation} A finer inter-class dissimilarity measure derived from the complete misclassification patterns shown in the non-diagonal elements of the confusion matrix results in two \(C*(C-1)\) dimensional flattened vectors \(\hat{p}\) and \(\hat{q}\), where each component is proportional to the counts of misclassifications from class \(i\) to class \(j\), is calculated as \begin{equation}\frac{e_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^C \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^C e_{ij}}, \quad \forall i, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}\end{equation} The resulting distances from both methods range from [0, 1], where we simply report a similarity measure given by $1- JSD(p,q)$.
elements.sent-2411-202: Error patterns for each model are encoded as binary vectors, where each entry corresponds to the correctness of a stimulus’s prediction.
elements.sent-2411-203: To compare the alignment between these binary vectors, we incorporate the following measures: Pearson Correlation Coefficient measures the linear correlation between the binary vectors of prediction correctness from two models, reflecting systematic agreement or disagreement in their prediction patterns over \( M \) shared testing stimuli.
elements.sent-2411-204: Cohen’s \(\kappa\) Score evaluates the agreement between two classifiers beyond chance.
elements.sent-2411-205: It is defined as: \begin{equation}\kappa_{xy} = \frac{c_{obs,xy} - c_{exp,xy}}{1 - c_{exp,xy}}\end{equation} \begin{equation}with  c_{exp,xy} = p_i p_j + (1 - p_i)(1 - p_j)\end{equation} where \( c_{obs,xy = \frac{\text{\# of agreements {M \), \( c_{exp,xy \) is the expected probability of agreement based on the accuracies \( p_x \) and \( p_y \) of two independent classifiers, and \( c_{obs,xy \) is the observed probability of agreement, offering a nuanced assessment of consistency across classifications.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Jaccard Similarity Coefficient quantifies the agreement between two binary classifiers' predictions, defined as: \begin{equation}J(x,y) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n x_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i + y_i - x_i y_i)}\end{equation} where \( x_i \) and \( y_i \) are binary indicators of the correctness (1) or incorrectness (0) for each \( i \)th prediction of two models.
elements.sent-2411-207: The numerator measures the intersection, or count of samples both models predict correctly, while the denominator measures the union, accounting for samples correctly predicted by either model.
elements.sent-2411-208: Hamming Distance measures the number of prediction discrepancies across a set of stimuli, defined as: \begin{equation}d(x, y) = \left| \{i : x_i \neq y_i, i = 1, \ldots, n\} \right|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-209: This metric counts the instances where the predictions from the two models differ.
elements.sent-2411-210: Agreement Score quantifies the normalized difference between agreement and disagreement counts in the prediction correctness by two models: \begin{equation}s(x, y) = \frac{(n_{11} + n_{00}) - (n_{10} + n_{01})}{n_{11} + n_{00} + n_{10} + n_{01}}\end{equation} where \( i, j \in \{0, 1\} \) and \( n_{ij \) represents the number of predictions where model \( x \) predicts \( i \) (correct/ incorrect) and model \( y \) predicts \( j \), across shared stimuli.}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.77\textwidth]{figs/dprimes} \caption{Discriminative ability (d' scores) of (top) representational and (bottom) behavioral similarity measures in distinguishing between trained vs. untrained models (left) and architectures (right). Each dot represents the average $d'$ score for a given dataset and metric, the dashed line indicates the overall mean $d'$ across all metrics.} \label{figs:fig2b}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{figs/mds_metric_corr.png} \caption{Consistency Between Similarity Metrics. (A) and (C) show the average correlation matrix and corresponding 2D multidimensional scaling (MDS) plot for behavioral similarity metrics, with distances defined as 1 minus the correlation. (B) and (D) present the same for representational similarity metrics.} \label{figs:fig3}
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figs/metric_gt_corr.png} \caption{Granular Comparison of Representational Similarity Measures with Behavioral Measures. (A) Average correlation between representational and behavioral metrics across datasets. (B) Distribution of correlation scores for each representational similarity measure with behavioral measures. Each point represents the averaged score for a dataset across all behavioral measures, with the dashed vertical line indicates the overall mean correlation across all metrics.} \label{figs:fig4}
elements.sent-2411-211: We analyze model behavior across a range of downstream tasks, spanning 20 behavioral datasets that include both in-distribution and various out-of-distribution (OOD) images and tasks (see Appendix): Label-Preserving OOD Datasets retain ImageNet labels but alter input distributions to test robustness (e.g., Stylized ImageNet, ImageNet silhouettes).
elements.sent-2411-212: Task-Shifting Datasets introduce new tasks and labels, requiring broader generalization (e.g., CelebA faces \citep{celebA , Oxford 102 Flower \citep{Flower ).}}
elements.sent-2411-213: We evaluated a diverse set of deep learning models pretrained on ImageNet-1k for 1000-class classification \citep{imagenet , including both convolutional networks (CNNs) and transformer-based models trained under supervised and self-supervised regimes.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Architectures included AlexNet \citep{alexnet , ResNet \citep{resnet2015 , VGG16 \citep{vgg , Inception \citep{inception , ResNeXt \citep{resnext , MoCo \citep{moco , ResNet Robust \citep{robustness , ViT-b16, ViT-ResNet, and Swin Transformer \citep{swin .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: Our analysis primarily targeted penultimate-layer representations, which are semantically aligned across models.
elements.sent-2411-216: For transformer models, we additionally examined final GELU activations.
elements.sent-2411-217: Randomized versions of AlexNet, ResNet, ViT, and Swin were included to assess untrained architectural behavior.
elements.sent-2411-218: See Appendix~\ref{appendix:layer_focus_rationale for details on our rationale for layer selection.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Other measures, like linear predictivity, which allow greater flexibility in aligning the two representations, showed limited ability in distinguishing between models trained with different architectures or trained from untrained models (see Appendix\ref{subsec:rsms for additional similarity matrices).}
elements.sent-2411-220: To quantify these distinctions, we computed $d'$ scores (Appendix\ref{subsec:dprime ) to assess each measure's ability to differentiate two categories of models: (a) those from different architectural families, and (b) those with varying levels of training (trained vs. untrained).}
elements.sent-2411-221: For instance, Procrustes achieved $d'$ scores with a mean of 3.73 when separating trained from untrained models across all datasets, while commonly used measures like CCA and linear predictivity produced much lower scores with means of 0.57 and 0.55, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-222: Similarly, some measures were better at discriminating architectural differences, with the soft-matching distance demonstrating the highest discriminability (mean of $d'$ scores = 1.61).
elements.sent-2411-223: Our results show that behavioral metrics effectively and consistently separate trained from untrained networks, with even the weakest metric (Confusion Matrix (JSD)) achieving a mean $d'$ of 1.68.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, most behavioral measures struggle to differentiate between architectural families (e.g., CNNs vs. Transformers), with the best-performing metric (Confusion Matrix (Inter-class JSD)) achieving an average $d'$ of 0.61 across all behavioral datasets (see Appendix\ref{subsec:behavmatrices for all similarity matrices).}
elements.sent-2411-225: This suggests that differences in these architectural motifs have minimal impact on model behavior (see Appendix\ref{appendix:architecture_behavior for further discussions).}
elements.sent-2411-226: As shown in Fig. \ref{figs:fig3 (Top), we find that behavioral metrics (mean r: $0.85 \pm 0.01$) are more correlated on average than representational metrics (mean r: $0.58 \pm 0.02$), with a significant difference $(z = -8.18, p = 2 \times 10^{-16 < 0.0001)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: How behavioral metrics distinguish models is crucial, as most comparative analyses of representations in neuroscience and AI revolve around understanding computations and how they relate to behavior; behaviorally grounded comparisons of model representations are key to this endeavor.
elements.sent-2411-228: We find that behavioral metrics distinguish between models consistently across different datasets, reinforcing the robustness of the model relationships they uncover.
elements.sent-2411-229: The consistency of the behavioral metrics—across datasets and with each other—fulfills another scientific desideratum of replicability.
elements.sent-2411-230: Given that we want to prioritize the model relationships uncovered by behavioral metrics, we move on to investigate which—if any—representational similarity metrics reveal the same underlying relationships between models.
elements.sent-2411-231: For each dataset, we computed the correlation between the model-by-model representational similarity matrix and the behavioral similarity matrix averaged across all behavioral metrics (Fig.~\ref{figs:fig4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-232: Three metrics stood out in their alignment with behavioral metrics-RSA (mean r: $0.53$), Linear CKA (mean r: $0.66$), and Procrustes (mean r: $0.70$).
elements.sent-2411-233: These same metrics also most strongly distinguished trained from untrained models (Fig.~\ref{figs:fig1 , top), each emphasizing global geometry or shape.}
elements.sent-2411-234: In contrast, common alternatives such as linear predictivity ($r = 0.30$) and CCA ($r = 0.22$) aligned more weakly with behavior.
elements.sent-2411-235: Given the opacity of internal representations, selecting representational similarity metrics can be challenging; these findings offer crucial guidance for metrics that support behaviorally grounded comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-236: In this study, we compared 8 neural representational similarity metrics and 9 behavioral measures across 20 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-237: Based on the premise that behavioral differences should be reflected in the representational structure of neural networks, we examined how well each metric aligns with behavior.
elements.sent-2411-238: Metrics such as RSA, CKA, and Procrustes distance—which preserve the overall geometry of neural representations—tend to align closely with behavioral measures.
elements.sent-2411-239: This likely stems from linear predictivity’s ability to map complex, distributed structures to simpler, compressed ones while still maintaining prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-240: For example, trained networks can predict untrained network activations with high symmetrized scores.
elements.sent-2411-241: This overly flexible nature has also been noted in recent work~\citep{khosla2024privileged, schaeffer2024position .}
elements.sent-2411-242: Nonetheless, linear predictivity remains valuable in applications such as BCIs, neural population control~\citep{bashivan2018neural , and in-silico hypothesis generation~\citep{jain2024computational .}}
elements.sent-2411-243: While behavioral measures are generally consistent with one another, representational similarity metrics vary widely, underscoring the need for a deeper understanding of how these metrics discriminate between models in practice.
elements.sent-2411-244: Our framework for selecting representational similarity metrics, while robust, rests on certain assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-245: It presumes a particular mechanism by which behavior is read out from internal representations.
elements.sent-2411-246: Different readout strategies—especially biologically inspired ones like sparse decoding—could yield different results, particularly if some models encode behaviorally relevant information in sparser or more localized ways.
elements.sent-2411-247: In such cases, the unit-level representational structure becomes crucial.
elements.sent-2411-248: Moreover, our evaluation focused on coarse model distinctions (e.g., trained vs. untrained, CNNs vs. transformers) and did not fully explore finer-grained variations such as subtle architectural tweaks or differences in initialization.
elements.sent-2411-249: Extending the framework to these subtler perturbations remains an important direction for future work.
elements.sent-2411-250: Further, our use of $d^{\prime $ to quantify model separability (which assumes Gaussian distributions) and Pearson correlations for behavioral vectors (which assume linearity) constitutes a statistical limitation; future work should explore alternatives such as Spearman rank correlations and other robust effect size measures.}
elements.sent-2411-251: Our desideratum involves selecting metrics which correlate with behavioral metrics to ensure that representational similarity metrics do not falsely indicate high internal similarity when behavioral differences are large.
elements.sent-2411-252: However, this does not imply that all representational similarity metrics should replicate behavioral metrics exactly.
elements.sent-2411-253: Overparameterized networks can achieve similar input–output mappings through distinct internal implementations, so a departure from a strict one-to-one correspondence between representations and behavior does not in itself detract from a metric’s validity.. \bibliographystyle{ccn_style .}
elements.sent-2411-254: Datasets are directly drawn from~\cite{geirhos2019, wang2019learning, geirhos2021partial , sharing the coarser 16 labels from ImageNet.}
elements.sent-2411-255: These datasets maintain the original classification task but introduce distribution shifts in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-256: These datasets introduce new tasks and labels, requiring models to generalize beyond the original ImageNet classification task.
elements.sent-2411-257: Each dataset consists of five trials, with non-overlapping subsets of classes selected per trial.
elements.sent-2411-258: Texture~\citep{cimpoi14describing : A texture classification dataset with 47 classes.}
elements.sent-2411-259: For each trial, 9 classes are selected, each containing 120 images, resulting in 1080 images per trial (864 for training, 216 for testing).
elements.sent-2411-260: This dataset evaluates the model’s capacity to classify texture patterns independent of object identity.
elements.sent-2411-261: Flower~\citep{Flower : A fine-grained classification dataset containing 102 flower species, each with a minimum of 40 images.}
elements.sent-2411-262: For each trial, 20 classes are selected, yielding 800 images per trial (640 for training, 160 for testing).
elements.sent-2411-263: This dataset assesses model generalization to fine-grained natural categories.
elements.sent-2411-264: Face (CelebA)~\citep{celebA : A face identity classification dataset designed to test model generalization in fine-grained recognition tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-265: For each trial, 50 identities are selected, each with 30 images, totaling 1500 images per trial.
elements.sent-2411-266: This dataset is of particular interest in cognitive science, given the specialized nature of face processing.
elements.subsubsection-2411-015: Raw Accuracy Differences Are Present but Not Central
elements.sent-2411-267: The primary goal of our behavioral evaluation is to assess similarity in error patterns — that is, whether models with comparable capabilities make similar mistakes.
elements.sent-2411-268: While transformer-based models consistently outperform CNNs in raw accuracy across both task-shift and label-preserving OOD tasks ($t = -6.41$, $p = 3.8 \times 10^{-6}$), raw accuracy alone does not capture how models behave.
elements.sent-2411-269: It reflects overall success rates but overlooks differences in the structure of errors.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our metrics are robust to variations in raw performance and are particularly effective for comparing models across a broad performance range.
elements.sent-2411-271: We also ensured that the datasets used exhibit sufficient variability in model accuracies, avoiding confounds due to ceiling or floor effects.
elements.sent-2411-272: Recent work~\cite{huang2024vits suggests that, despite differing inductive biases, CNNs and ViTs often converge toward similar functional solutions during training.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Consistent with this, we find that models with distinct architectures can nonetheless exhibit comparable patterns of generalization and error when trained on the same task.
elements.sent-2411-274: This convergence helps explain why architectural differences may have limited impact on behavioral error structure once task competence is achieved.
elements.subsection-2411-014: Layer Selection and Controlled Feature Extraction
elements.sent-2411-275: While focusing only on penultimate layers in constructing model-by-model representational similarity matrices may appear limiting, this decision was grounded in both practical and theoretical considerations.
elements.sent-2411-276: Penultimate layers provide stable, semantically aligned representations that directly influence output behavior, making them a consistent and interpretable basis for comparison across diverse architectures.
elements.sent-2411-277: Penultimate layers directly influence model outputs and thus provide behaviorally meaningful signals.
elements.sent-2411-278: Unlike earlier layers, which capture low-level features, these final representations are more interpretable and better aligned with task-level decisions.
elements.sent-2411-279: Comparing intermediate layers across CNNs and Transformers is challenging due to architectural differences.
elements.sent-2411-280: CNNs produce spatial maps, while ViTs output token sequences, often with a [CLS] token.
elements.sent-2411-281: In penultimate layers, these differences are easier to normalize (e.g., [CLS] or average pooling for ViTs, global pooling for CNNs).
elements.sent-2411-282: Intermediate layers, however, require arbitrary choices about which token or spatial location to use, making alignment noisier.
elements.sent-2411-283: To address concerns that penultimate layers may overemphasize training-related effects, we extended our analysis to include intermediate representations sampled via normalized depth indexing.
elements.sent-2411-284: Layers at normalized indices 0.6, 0.7, and 0.8 were selected to capture increasingly abstract but pre-output stages.
elements.sent-2411-285: For each model, we filtered for core representational modules (e.g., Conv2d, Linear, attn.proj, mlp.fc2) and selected the nearest valid layer to each index.
elements.sent-2411-286: To control feature dimensionality, we applied a consistent feature extraction method: for CNNs, we used the center spatial location across all channels; for ViTs, we used the center token (excluding the [CLS] token when present).
elements.sent-2411-287: This approach avoids global pooling and keeps the features spatially specific, which is important for metrics like soft-matching.
elements.sent-2411-288: Results Across all Label-Preserving OOD Datasets and multiple intermediate depths, we observed strong correlations between the representational similarity matrices of intermediate layers and those of the penultimate layer (mean $r = 0.86 \pm 0.003$), compared to a baseline correlation computed between different metrics at the same depth ($r = 0.73 \pm 0.006$).
elements.sent-2411-289: This suggests a degree of hierarchical consistency within models — that is, model-to-model representational similarity remains relatively stable across a range of layer choices (see Table~\ref{tab:avg_corr ).}
elements.figure-2411-020: \caption{Average correlation across datasets} \label{tab:avg_corr} 0.9\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lccc} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Normalized Layer Index} 0.6 0.7 0.8 RSA 0.83 0.80 0.87 CCA 0.89 0.89 0.91 Linear CKA 0.80 0.79 0.78 Procrustes 0.84 0.84 0.85 Linear Predictivity 0.84 0.79 0.85 Semi-matching 0.89 0.88 0.91 Soft-matching 0.88 0.88 0.89 Mutual k-NN (10) 0.90 0.89 0.87 Mutual k-NN (20) 0.87 0.87 0.85 Mutual k-NN (50) 0.86 0.85 0.82 Mutual k-NN (100) 0.86 0.85 0.83 Mutual k-NN (200) 0.89 0.86 0.87 Layer Mean $0.86 \pm 0.009$ $0.85 \pm 0.01$ $0.86 \pm 0.01$ Baseline Mean $0.74 \pm 0.02$ $0.72 \pm 0.02$ $0.74 \pm 0.02$ \end{tabular} }
elements.subsection-2411-015: Controlling Feature Dimensionality in Soft-Matching Analyses
elements.sent-2411-290: A potential concern is that soft-matching’s sensitivity to architectural differences may stem from variation in feature dimensionality, as transformers often have larger or more variable penultimate-layer sizes (Table~\ref{tab:feature_dims ).}
elements.sent-2411-291: To control for this, we re-evaluated soft-matching by randomly subsampling 768 units—the smallest shared dimensionality—across all models.
elements.sent-2411-292: Sampling was repeated over five trials and results averaged.
elements.sent-2411-293: Even with equalized dimensions, soft-matching consistently distinguished CNNs from transformers on the Task-Shifting Datasets.
elements.sent-2411-294: The controlled and original similarity matrices remained highly correlated ($r = 0.997$), suggesting that the observed separation reflects genuine representational differences rather than dimensionality artifacts.
elements.figure-2411-021: \caption{Penultimate layer feature dimensionality across all evaluated models} \label{tab:feature_dims} 0.6\linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{ll} Model / Layer Feature Size AlexNet 4096 ResNet 2048 VGG16 4096 Inception 2048 ResNeXt 2048 MoCo 2048 ResNet Robust 2048 ViT-ResNet 768 ViT-ResNet GELU 3072 ViT Final 768 ViT GELU Final 3072 Swin 1024 Swin GELU 4096 \end{tabular} }
